meta.notes: Same Saylor 'General Chemistry I MCQ' QuizOver PDF (CC BY-NC-ND 3.0) as cpbs-genchem-05. The PDF withholds the answer key (links to QuizOver.com), so all items are answerProvenance=verified-from-source-text with answers derived from established VSEPR/bonding rules, valence-electron counts, and gas laws. Choices reproduced verbatim in printed order, labeled A-D. Selected to add molecular-geometry/shape coverage (linear, trigonal bipyramidal, bent, etc.) and electronegativity trends not present in the GOB-derived files; deliberately avoided duplicating earlier bonding items. LICENSE NOTE: open but NC + ND; confirm rights before any commercial shipping.

questions.stem: A sample of a certain gas has a volume of 222 mL at 695 mm Hg and 0 °C. What would be the volume of this same sample of gas if it were measured at 333 mm Hg and 0 °C?
questions.explanation: Temperature is constant, so by Boyle's law V2 = V1 × P1/P2 = 222 mL × (695 mm Hg / 333 mm Hg) = 463 mL. Lowering the pressure increases the volume.

hints.prompt: At constant temperature, how are a gas's pressure and volume related?
hints.rationale: Boyle's law: pressure times volume is constant at fixed temperature.

hints.prompt: Solving P1V1 = P2V2 for V2 with V1 = 222 mL, P1 = 695, and P2 = 333, which setup is right?
Halving the pressure should roughly double the volume (to about 460 mL). Which two choices can be eliminated as inconsistent with that estimate?
hints.rationale: A pressure drop from 695 to 333 (about half) roughly doubles 222 mL to about 460 mL, so 359 mL (too small) and 894 mL (too large) are off.

questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. The source PDF withholds the answer key; answer independently verified by calculation.

questions.stem: For a given sample of gas molecules, the average kinetic energy depends only on the value of the ________________.
questions.explanation: The average translational kinetic energy of gas molecules is directly proportional to the absolute temperature (KE_avg = 3/2 kT). It does not depend on pressure, volume, or the number of moles.

hints.prompt: The average kinetic energy of ideal-gas molecules is INDEPENDENT of which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The pressure and the volume of the gas

choices.label: B
choices.text: The absolute temperature of the gas

choices.label: C
choices.text: The average speed of its molecules

hints.rationale: At a given temperature, average kinetic energy does not depend on the gas's pressure or volume.

hints.prompt: If you compress a fixed sample of gas (raising the pressure and lowering the volume) without heating or cooling it, what happens to the average kinetic energy of its molecules?
hints.rationale: Average kinetic energy is unchanged by pressure or volume when the gas is neither heated nor cooled.

hints.prompt: Which two choices can be eliminated because average kinetic energy is independent of them?
hints.rationale: Average molecular kinetic energy depends only on temperature, not on pressure or volume.

questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. The source PDF withholds the answer key; answer independently verified.

questions.stem: The phase change from solid to gas is called ______________.
questions.explanation: Sublimation is the direct conversion of a solid to a gas without passing through the liquid phase (e.g., dry ice). Boiling and evaporation are liquid-to-gas transitions.

hints.prompt: Can a solid ever turn into a gas without first melting into a liquid?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes - some solids convert directly to a gas

choices.label: B
choices.text: No - a solid must always melt to a liquid first

choices.label: C
choices.text: No - gases can never come from solids

choices.label: D
choices.text: Only liquids can ever become gases

hints.rationale: Some substances go straight from solid to gas without passing through a liquid stage.

hints.prompt: Boiling and evaporation both describe which phase transition (so they cannot be solid-to-gas)?
hints.rationale: Boiling and evaporation are liquid-to-gas transitions.

hints.prompt: Which two choices can be eliminated because they describe a LIQUID turning into a gas, not a solid?
hints.rationale: Boiling and evaporation start from a liquid, not a solid.

questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-ND 3.0 (The Saylor Foundation; NonCommercial, NoDerivatives)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. The source PDF withholds the answer key; answer independently verified (definitional).

questions.stem: According to Valence Shell Electron Pair Repulsion Theory (VSEPR), what number of bonded atoms is normally expected to produce a tetrahedral geometry?
questions.explanation: A central atom with four bonding electron domains and no lone pairs adopts a tetrahedral geometry, with ideal bond angles of 109.5°.

hints.prompt: In VSEPR theory, what determines a molecule's shape?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The number of electron domains (bonding groups plus lone pairs) around the central atom

choices.label: D
choices.text: The number of neutrons in the atoms

hints.rationale: Molecular geometry is set by the count of electron domains around the central atom.

hints.prompt: What is the ideal bond angle of a tetrahedral geometry, distinguishing it from linear (180) or trigonal planar (120)?
hints.rationale: A tetrahedral geometry has bond angles of about 109.5 degrees.

hints.prompt: Which two choices can be eliminated because they give trigonal-planar (three) or octahedral (six) shapes, not tetrahedral?
hints.rationale: Three domains give trigonal planar and six give octahedral, neither of which is tetrahedral.

questions.stem: Based on the Valence Shell Electron Pair Repulsion Theory (VSEPR), what is the molecular geometry of BeCl2?
questions.explanation: Beryllium in BeCl2 has only two bonding domains and no lone pairs, so the molecule is linear (Cl-Be-Cl angle = 180°).

hints.prompt: In VSEPR theory, what determines a molecule's geometry?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The number of electron domains - bonding groups plus lone pairs - around the central atom

choices.label: B
choices.text: The total mass of the molecule

choices.label: D
choices.text: The number of neutrons in the atoms

hints.rationale: Molecular geometry is set by the number of electron domains around the central atom.

hints.prompt: Beryllium in BeCl2 has how many bonding domains and lone pairs?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Two bonding domains and zero lone pairs

choices.label: C
choices.text: Three bonding domains and one lone pair

choices.label: D
choices.text: Two bonding domains and two lone pairs

hints.rationale: Be forms two bonds with no lone pairs.

hints.prompt: With only two bonding domains, which two geometries can be eliminated as requiring four or five domains?
hints.rationale: Tetrahedral needs four domains and trigonal bipyramidal needs five; BeCl2 has only two.

questions.stem: Based on the Valence Shell Electron Pair Repulsion Theory (VSEPR), what is the molecular geometry of PI5?
questions.explanation: Phosphorus in PI5 has five bonding domains and no lone pairs, giving a trigonal bipyramidal geometry.

hints.prompt: In VSEPR theory, what determines a molecule's geometry?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The number of electron domains - bonding groups plus lone pairs - around the central atom

choices.label: B
choices.text: The total mass of the molecule

choices.label: D
choices.text: The number of neutrons in the atoms

hints.rationale: Molecular geometry is set by the number of electron domains around the central atom.

hints.prompt: Phosphorus in PI5 is bonded to how many atoms, and has how many lone pairs?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Five bonds and no lone pairs

choices.label: D
choices.text: Three bonds and one lone pair

hints.rationale: Phosphorus forms five P-I bonds with no lone pairs.

hints.prompt: With five bonding domains, which two geometries can be eliminated as requiring only two or three domains?
hints.rationale: Linear needs two domains and trigonal planar needs three; PI5 has five.

questions.stem: Based on the Valence Shell Electron Pair Repulsion Theory (VSEPR), what is the molecular geometry of CF4?
questions.explanation: Carbon in CF4 has four bonding domains and no lone pairs, giving a tetrahedral geometry (bond angles 109.5°).

hints.prompt: In VSEPR theory, what determines a molecule's geometry?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The number of electron domains - bonding groups plus lone pairs - around the central atom

choices.label: B
choices.text: The total mass of the molecule

choices.label: D
choices.text: The number of neutrons in the atoms

hints.rationale: Molecular geometry is set by the number of electron domains around the central atom.

hints.prompt: Carbon in CF4 forms how many bonds, and has how many lone pairs?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Four bonds and no lone pairs

choices.label: C
choices.text: Three bonds and one lone pair

hints.rationale: Carbon forms four C-F bonds with no lone pairs.

hints.prompt: With four bonding domains, which two geometries can be eliminated as requiring two or five domains?
hints.rationale: Linear needs two domains and trigonal bipyramidal needs five; CF4 has four.

questions.stem: Based on the Valence Shell Electron Pair Repulsion Theory (VSEPR), which of the following corresponds most closely to the geometry of the IBr2- ion?
questions.explanation: In IBr2- the central iodine has 2 bonding pairs and 3 lone pairs (5 electron domains). The three lone pairs occupy the equatorial positions of a trigonal bipyramid, leaving the two I-Br bonds axial, so the ion is linear.

hints.prompt: How do lone pairs on a central atom affect the molecular geometry versus the electron geometry?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Lone pairs occupy domains but are not counted in the molecular shape

choices.label: B
choices.text: Lone pairs are counted as if they were atoms

choices.label: C
choices.text: Lone pairs never have any effect

choices.label: D
choices.text: Lone pairs make every molecule bent

hints.rationale: The molecular shape counts only bonded atoms, but where the lone pairs sit still matters.

hints.prompt: In IBr2-, iodine has 2 bonding pairs and 3 lone pairs (five domains). Where do the 3 lone pairs go in a trigonal bipyramid, and where does that leave the two bonds?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Lone pairs go equatorial; the bonds go axial

choices.label: B
choices.text: Lone pairs go axial; the bonds go equatorial

choices.label: D
choices.text: Lone pairs and bonds alternate at random

hints.rationale: The three lone pairs take the equatorial plane, leaving the two I-Br bonds axial.

hints.prompt: If the two I-Br bonds sit at the axial positions (180 degrees apart), which two 'bent' geometries can be eliminated?
hints.rationale: Axial bonds that are 180 degrees apart cannot give a bent angle of 120 or 109.5 degrees.

questions.sourceName: QuizOver.com / The Saylor Foundation — General Chemistry I MCQ (Smithback & Lerebours), Unit 03: Bonding

questions.stem: Based on the Valence Shell Electron Pair Repulsion Theory (VSEPR), which of the following corresponds most closely to the molecular geometry of SF2?
questions.explanation: Sulfur in SF2 has 2 bonding pairs and 2 lone pairs (4 electron domains, tetrahedral electron geometry), giving a bent molecular shape with a bond angle near 109.5°.

hints.prompt: In VSEPR theory, what determines a molecule's geometry?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The number of electron domains - bonding groups plus lone pairs - around the central atom

choices.label: B
choices.text: The total mass of the molecule

choices.label: D
choices.text: The number of neutrons in the atoms

hints.rationale: Molecular geometry is set by the number of electron domains around the central atom.

hints.prompt: How many total electron domains surround sulfur in SF2, counting both bonds and lone pairs?
hints.rationale: Two bonds plus two lone pairs make four electron domains.

hints.prompt: SF2 has four electron domains. Which two shapes can be eliminated because they do not arise from a four-domain (tetrahedral-based) center?
hints.rationale: Linear comes from two domains and T-shaped from five; SF2's four domains give a bent shape.

questions.stem: Eighteen electrons are present in the Lewis structure of which of the following molecules?
questions.explanation: Total valence electrons: SO2 = 6 + 2(6) = 18. By comparison, N2O = 2(5) + 6 = 16, CO2 = 4 + 2(6) = 16, and HCN = 1 + 4 + 5 = 10.

hints.prompt: How do you count the total valence electrons for a molecule's Lewis structure?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Count only the number of bonds

choices.label: D
choices.text: Multiply the number of atoms by 2

hints.rationale: The total is the sum of every atom's valence electrons.

hints.prompt: For CO2, carbon contributes 4 and each oxygen 6. How many valence electrons does its Lewis structure have?
hints.rationale: 4 + 2 x 6 = 16 for CO2.

hints.prompt: Which two molecules can be eliminated because their valence-electron totals (10 and 16) are not 18?
hints.rationale: HCN has 10 and CO2 has 16 valence electrons, neither of which is 18.

questions.sourceName: QuizOver.com / The Saylor Foundation — General Chemistry I MCQ (Smithback & Lerebours), Unit 03: Bonding
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-ND 3.0 (The Saylor Foundation; NonCommercial, NoDerivatives)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. The source PDF withholds the answer key; answer independently verified by valence-electron count.

questions.stem: How many p orbitals are there (in a given p subshell)?
questions.explanation: A p subshell contains three orbitals (px, py, pz), oriented along the three perpendicular axes; together they hold up to six electrons.

hints.prompt: What does an electron subshell (such as s, p, or d) consist of?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A set of orbitals, each able to hold up to 2 electrons

choices.label: D
choices.text: Exactly eight orbitals in every case

hints.rationale: A subshell is a group of orbitals, and each orbital holds at most two electrons.

hints.prompt: A p subshell holds a maximum of 6 electrons, and each orbital holds up to 2 electrons. How do you find the number of p orbitals?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Take 6 directly as the number of orbitals

hints.rationale: Number of orbitals = maximum electrons divided by 2 (six divided by two).

hints.prompt: Which two choices can be eliminated - one being the count for an s subshell, the other a number no subshell has?
hints.rationale: An s subshell has 1 orbital and no subshell has exactly 4; the p subshell is different.

questions.sourceName: QuizOver.com / The Saylor Foundation — General Chemistry I MCQ (Smithback & Lerebours), Unit 03: Bonding
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-ND 3.0 (The Saylor Foundation; NonCommercial, NoDerivatives)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim (parenthetical clarifier added; original stem 'How many p orbitals are there?'). The source PDF withholds the answer key; answer independently verified.

questions.stem: In which of the following pairs of molecules do both members of the pair have the same molecular geometry?
questions.explanation: H2O and OF2 each have a central atom with two bonding pairs and two lone pairs, so both are bent. In the other pairs the members differ (SO2 bent vs CO2 linear; H2S bent vs HCN linear; NH3 trigonal pyramidal vs SO3 trigonal planar).

hints.prompt: What determines whether two molecules share the same molecular geometry?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Having the same number of bonding pairs and lone pairs on the central atom

hints.rationale: Identical bonding-pair and lone-pair counts give the same shape.

hints.prompt: In the pair SO2 and CO2, what are the two shapes?

choices.label: A
choices.text: SO2 is bent and CO2 is linear, so they differ

hints.rationale: SO2 (with a lone pair) is bent while CO2 is linear, so their geometries differ.

hints.prompt: Which two pairs can be eliminated because one member is bent while the other is linear?

choices.label: A
choices.text: SO2 and CO2; H2S and HCN

choices.label: B
choices.text: H2O and OF2; NH3 and SO3

choices.label: C
choices.text: SO2 and CO2; H2O and OF2

choices.label: D
choices.text: NH3 and SO3; H2O and OF2

hints.rationale: In each of these pairs one molecule is bent and the other linear, so their geometries differ.

questions.stem: What is the approximate H-B-H angle in BH3?
questions.explanation: BH3 is trigonal planar (three bonding domains, no lone pairs on boron), so the H-B-H bond angles are about 120°.

hints.prompt: What sets the bond angles in a molecule?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Its electron-domain geometry (for example linear, trigonal planar, or tetrahedral)

choices.label: B
choices.text: The atomic masses of the atoms

hints.rationale: Bond angles follow from the molecule's electron-domain geometry.

hints.prompt: Boron in BH3 has three bonding domains and no lone pairs, giving which geometry?
hints.rationale: Three bonds and no lone pairs give a trigonal planar shape.

hints.prompt: Which two angles can be eliminated because they belong to tetrahedral (109) or linear (180) shapes, not trigonal planar?
hints.rationale: 109 degrees is tetrahedral and 180 degrees is linear; a trigonal-planar molecule is neither.

questions.sourceName: QuizOver.com / The Saylor Foundation — General Chemistry I MCQ (Smithback & Lerebours), Unit 03: Bonding
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-ND 3.0 (The Saylor Foundation; NonCommercial, NoDerivatives)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim (original option 3 printed '120'; normalized to 120°). The source PDF withholds the answer key; answer independently verified.

questions.stem: What is the hybridization of the oxygen atom in water?

choices.label: D
choices.text: The oxygen atom does not hybridize in water.

questions.explanation: Oxygen in water has two bonding pairs and two lone pairs (four electron domains), which corresponds to sp3 hybridization and a bent molecular shape.

hints.prompt: What determines the hybridization of a central atom?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Its number of electron domains - bonding groups plus lone pairs

hints.rationale: Hybridization follows from the count of electron domains around the atom.

hints.prompt: Oxygen in water has 2 bonds and 2 lone pairs. How many total electron domains is that?
hints.rationale: Two bonds plus two lone pairs make four domains.

hints.prompt: Which two choices can be eliminated - one implying only two domains, the other denying hybridization altogether?

choices.label: A
choices.text: sp and 'the oxygen atom does not hybridize in water'

choices.label: D
choices.text: sp3 and 'the oxygen atom does not hybridize in water'

hints.rationale: sp implies just two domains, and oxygen's bonding does involve hybrid orbitals, so both are wrong.

questions.stem: Which molecule contains a double bond?
questions.explanation: Phosgene, COCl2, has a carbon-oxygen double bond (C=O) plus two C-Cl single bonds. SF2 and CF4 contain only single bonds.

hints.prompt: Which pairing of atoms commonly forms a double bond in these small molecules?
hints.rationale: Carbon-oxygen double bonds are common, as in carbonyl groups.

hints.prompt: In SF2 and CF4, how are the atoms bonded?
hints.rationale: SF2 and CF4 contain only single bonds.

hints.prompt: Which two choices can be eliminated because they contain only single bonds?
hints.rationale: Both SF2 and CF4 have only single bonds, so neither contains a double bond.

questions.stem: Which of the following best describes the variation of the electronegativity of the elements with respect to their position on the periodic table?

choices.label: A
choices.text: increases across a period; increases down a group

choices.label: B
choices.text: increases across a period; decreases down a group

choices.label: C
choices.text: decreases across a period; increases down a group

choices.label: D
choices.text: decreases across a period; decreases down a group

questions.explanation: Electronegativity increases from left to right across a period (increasing nuclear charge, smaller atoms) and decreases down a group (valence electrons farther from the nucleus, more shielding).

hints.prompt: What is electronegativity?

choices.label: A
choices.text: An atom's tendency to attract the shared electrons in a bond

choices.label: C
choices.text: The number of neutrons in an atom

choices.label: D
choices.text: The physical size (volume) of an atom

hints.rationale: Electronegativity is an atom's ability to attract shared bonding electrons.

hints.prompt: Across a period, atoms get smaller with higher nuclear charge. What does that do to electronegativity?

choices.label: C
choices.text: It does not change across the period

choices.label: D
choices.text: It changes only down a group

hints.rationale: Higher nuclear charge and smaller size raise electronegativity across a period.

hints.prompt: Given that electronegativity increases across a period, which two choices can be eliminated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'decreases across a period; increases down a group' and 'decreases across a period; decreases down a group'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'increases across a period; decreases down a group' and 'increases across a period; increases down a group'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'increases across a period; increases down a group' and 'decreases across a period; increases down a group'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'increases across a period; decreases down a group' and 'decreases across a period; decreases down a group'

hints.rationale: Both 'decreases across a period' options contradict the known increase across a period.

questions.stem: Which of the following combinations of two elements is most likely to produce highly ionic bonds?
questions.explanation: Ionic character increases with the electronegativity difference between the elements. Lithium (a low-electronegativity metal) combined with fluorine (the most electronegative element) gives the most ionic bond. The other pairs are nonmetal-nonmetal (covalent).

hints.prompt: What makes a bond more ionic?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A large electronegativity difference (a metal with a very electronegative nonmetal)

hints.rationale: Ionic character grows with the electronegativity difference between the atoms.

hints.prompt: Three of the choices pair two nonmetals. What kind of bonding do nonmetal-nonmetal pairs give?
hints.rationale: Nonmetal-nonmetal combinations bond covalently.

hints.prompt: Which two choices can be eliminated because both elements are nonmetals (favoring covalent, not ionic, bonds)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: nitrogen and oxygen; boron and nitrogen

choices.label: B
choices.text: nitrogen and fluorine; lithium and fluorine

choices.label: C
choices.text: nitrogen and oxygen; lithium and fluorine

choices.label: D
choices.text: boron and nitrogen; lithium and fluorine

hints.rationale: Nitrogen-oxygen and boron-nitrogen are nonmetal pairs that bond covalently.

questions.sourceName: QuizOver.com / The Saylor Foundation — General Chemistry I MCQ (Smithback & Lerebours), Unit 03: Bonding
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-ND 3.0 (The Saylor Foundation; NonCommercial, NoDerivatives)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. The source PDF withholds the answer key; answer independently verified.

questions.stem: Which of the following combinations of two elements is most likely to produce covalent bonds?
questions.explanation: Covalent bonds form between two nonmetals of similar electronegativity. Nitrogen and oxygen are both nonmetals, so they bond covalently. The other pairs each contain a reactive metal (Na or Li) with a nonmetal, favoring ionic bonding.

hints.prompt: What kind of element pair forms covalent bonds?

choices.label: D
choices.text: A noble gas and a metal

hints.rationale: Covalent bonds form between nonmetals.

hints.prompt: Sodium and lithium are reactive metals. Pairing a metal with a nonmetal favors which bond type?
hints.rationale: A metal combined with a nonmetal gives ionic bonding.

hints.prompt: Which two choices can be eliminated because they pair a reactive metal with a nonmetal (favoring ionic bonds)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: sodium and fluorine; lithium and fluorine

choices.label: B
choices.text: nitrogen and oxygen; sodium and nitrogen

choices.label: C
choices.text: nitrogen and oxygen; lithium and fluorine

choices.label: D
choices.text: sodium and nitrogen; nitrogen and oxygen

hints.rationale: Sodium-fluorine and lithium-fluorine combine a metal with a nonmetal, giving ionic bonds.

questions.sourceName: QuizOver.com / The Saylor Foundation — General Chemistry I MCQ (Smithback & Lerebours), Unit 03: Bonding
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-ND 3.0 (The Saylor Foundation; NonCommercial, NoDerivatives)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. The source PDF withholds the answer key; answer independently verified.

questions.stem: Which of the following elements is most likely to participate in the formation of multiple bonds?
questions.explanation: Among these, sulfur most readily forms multiple (double) bonds, as in SO2 and SO3. Hydrogen forms only a single bond, sodium bonds ionically as a metal, and chlorine typically forms single bonds.

hints.prompt: What must two atoms do to form a multiple (double or triple) bond?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Share two or three pairs of electrons between them

choices.label: B
choices.text: Have one atom completely transfer its electrons to the other

choices.label: D
choices.text: Share only a single pair of electrons

hints.rationale: A multiple bond means two (double) or three (triple) shared pairs of electrons.

hints.prompt: Hydrogen forms only a single bond and sodium bonds ionically as a metal. Which of these can be ruled out as multiple-bond formers?

choices.label: D
choices.text: None of them can be ruled out

hints.rationale: Hydrogen forms only a single bond and sodium is an ionic metal.

hints.prompt: Which two choices can be eliminated - one that forms only a single bond, and one that bonds ionically as a metal?
hints.rationale: Hydrogen forms only single bonds and sodium is a metal that bonds ionically.

questions.stem: Which of the following elements is most likely to form compounds involving an expanded valence shell of electrons?
questions.explanation: Phosphorus is a period-3 element with accessible d orbitals, so it can exceed an octet (e.g., PCl5, PF6-). Period-2 atoms O and N cannot expand their valence shell, and Na is a metal that loses electrons.

hints.prompt: Why can some atoms hold more than eight valence electrons (an expanded octet)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They are in period 3 or lower, which gives them access to d orbitals

choices.label: D
choices.text: They are metals that lose all of their electrons

hints.rationale: An expanded octet requires the d orbitals available to period-3 and heavier atoms.

hints.prompt: Oxygen and nitrogen are period-2 atoms. Can they expand their valence shell?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No - period-2 atoms are limited to an octet

hints.rationale: Period-2 atoms cannot exceed an octet.

hints.prompt: Which two choices can be eliminated because they are period-2 atoms that cannot exceed an octet?
hints.rationale: Oxygen and nitrogen are second-period elements limited to eight valence electrons.
